title: Repetitive Questions
description: One entry per repeated stem (normalized match on question text + correct answer, 2+ appearances across exams). Underscores/blanks and light stem variation collapse when the keyed answer matches; instanceCount and appearances record every exam slot. Ordered by instanceCount ascending (2, then 3, then 4…); within the same count: True/False before MCQ, then 2019→2021→2024→2025, then exam question id (Q1, Q2, Q1.a…).

questions.questionText: Implementation of the chosen alternative is the final step in the decision-making process.
questions.explanation: According to the rational decision-making process model, implementing the chosen alternative is the fourth step. The fifth and final step is gathering problem-related feedback to determine the effect of the implemented alternative on the identified problem.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 187: Figure 8.3 shows Step 4 is 'Implement chosen alternative' and Step 5 is 'Gather problem-related feedback.'

questions.questionText: Bureaucracy can be characterized as a management system with detailed procedures, rules, and a clearly outlined organizational hierarchy with impersonal relationships among organization members.
questions.explanation: Max Weber's concept of bureaucracy is precisely defined by these exact characteristics. It is a system intended to increase organizational efficiency through strict adherence to rules, formal hierarchies, and impersonal interactions.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 252: "Bureaucracy is the term Max Weber used to describe a management system that includes three primary components: detailed procedures and rules, a clearly outlined organizational hierarchy, and impersonal relationships among organization members."

questions.questionText: Communication is considered a supplementary management skill rather than a fundamental management skill.
questions.explanation: The textbook indicates that chief executives rank communication skills extremely high—tying for first place as the most important skill to teach management students. Thus, it is a fundamental, not supplementary, management skill.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 351: "Table 15.1 shows that chief executives rank communication skills... tying for rank 1 as the most important skill that should be taught to management students."

questions.questionText: In order to preserve their position power, managers should avoid doing personal favors for subordinates.
questions.explanation: Doing personal favors is a strategy to develop a sense of obligation toward the manager, which increases their personal power, not their position power. Position power is derived from the formal organizational position a manager holds, so doing personal favors does not diminish or preserve it.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 508: "To increase personal power, a manager should attempt to develop the following attitudes and beliefs in other organization members: 1. A sense of obligation toward the manager... The basic strategy suggested for creating this sense of obligation is to do personal favors for people."

questions.questionText: Human skills, unlike technical skills, involve the ability to see the organization as a whole.
questions.explanation: The ability to see the organization as a whole is the definition of 'Conceptual Skills'. 'Human Skills' are defined as the ability to build cooperation and work effectively with people.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 11: "Conceptual skills involve the ability to see the organization as a whole... Human skills build cooperation within the team being led."

questions.questionText: Groupthink is a group decision-making process in which negative feedback on any suggested alternative by any group member is forbidden until all members have presented alternatives that they perceive as valuable.
questions.explanation: Groupthink is not a structured process where feedback is forbidden; rather, it is a psychological phenomenon where "the desire for agreement so dominates the group that it overrides the need to realistically appraise alternative problem solutions." It results in members censoring their own ideas to conform.
questions.reference: Chapter 18: Groups and Teams - Found on Slide 16.

questions.questionText: Span of management refers to the number of organizational levels below that of a particular manager's.
questions.explanation: Span of management (also called span of control) refers to the number of individuals a manager directly supervises, not the number of vertical organizational levels below them.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 262: "Span of management—also called span of control, span of authority, span of supervision, and span of responsibility—is the number of individuals a manager supervises."

questions.questionText: Wikstrom's human resource inventory forms are meant to be used exclusively for filling managerial positions and cannot be used for filling nonmanagerial positions.
questions.explanation: While Wikstrom's specific examples (like the Management Inventory Card) focus heavily on management personnel, the textbook explicitly notes that similar forms can be used to keep a human resource inventory of nonmanagerial personnel as well.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 299: "As you can see, Wikstrom’s forms... are focused on management personnel. Of course, similar forms could be used to keep a human resource inventory of nonmanagerial personnel."

questions.questionText: The basic elements of the interpersonal communication process are the source, the encoder, and the decoder.
questions.explanation: The statement is false because it omits the "signal." The three basic elements of the interpersonal communication process are the source/encoder, the signal, and the decoder/destination.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 348: "To be a successful interpersonal communicator, a manager must understand the three basic elements of interpersonal communication: the source/encoder, the signal, and the decoder/destination."

questions.questionText: Henri Fayol recommends that businesses establish multiple guiding authorities when organizing resources.
questions.explanation: Henri Fayol's principle of unity of command explicitly states that an individual should have only one boss. Establishing multiple guiding authorities contradicts this rule.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 260: "Fayol’s management principle of unity of command is the rationale for the existence of the scalar relationship. This principle recommends that an individual have only one boss."

questions.questionText: Vertical dimensioning is directly related to the concept of the scalar relationship that is, the chain of command.
questions.explanation: Vertical dimensioning deals with the levels of authority within an organization. This is directly built on the scalar relationship (chain of command), which traces authority from the highest level down to the lowest.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 260: "Vertical dimensioning refers to the extent to which an organization uses vertical levels to separate job responsibilities. Vertical dimensioning is directly related to the concept of the scalar relationship—that is, the chain of command."

questions.questionText: _________ is the assignment of various portions of a particular task among a number of organization members.
questions.explanation: Division of labor is defined explicitly as the assignment of various portions of a particular task among a number of organization members to increase specialization and efficiency.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 252: "Division of labor is the assignment of various portions of a particular task among a number of organization members."

questions.questionText: Which of the following is an advantage of planning?
questions.explanation: One of the key advantages of planning is that it enhances decision coordination across the organization by focusing managers on the future and overarching objectives.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 162: "Advantages of planning include... it enhances decision coordination; and it emphasizes organizational objectives."

questions.questionText: _________ is managerial activity aimed at bringing organizational performance up to the level of performance standards.
questions.explanation: Corrective action is the specific managerial activity in the controlling process aimed at correcting mistakes and bringing organizational performance up to the level of the established performance standards.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 506: "Corrective action is managerial activity aimed at bringing organizational performance up to the level of performance standards."

questions.questionText: _________ is the systematic effort to compare performance to predetermined standards in order to determine whether performance is in line with those standards, or needs to be corrected.
questions.explanation: Roberto Mockler's formal definition of controlling explicitly states it is a systematic effort by business management to compare performance to predetermined standards to determine whether performance is in line with those standards.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 502: "According to Roberto Mockler, controlling is a systematic effort by business management to compare performance to predetermined standards, plans, or objectives to determine whether performance is in line with these standards..."

questions.questionText: The more resources unused during the production process, the more ________ the manager.
questions.explanation: Managerial efficiency refers to the proportion of total organizational resources that contribute to productivity. Therefore, the textbook explicitly states that if more resources are unused or wasted, the manager is considered inefficient.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 10: "The more resources wasted or unused during the production process, the more inefficient is the manager."

questions.questionText: This is an example of a(n)
questions.context: Trendy Garb is a retail store that sells women's apparel. The store has comprehensive rules, policies, and procedures for matters relating to customer complaints and product returns. Hence, when faced with either of these situations, the store manager simply needs to refer to these guidelines and proceed accordingly.
questions.explanation: Because the store has comprehensive, standard operating procedures, rules, and policies established for routine situations like returns and complaints, the manager is executing a programmed decision.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 182: "A programmed decision is routine and repetitive, and the organization typically develops specific ways to handle such decisions." Table 8.1 also lists 'Standard operating procedures' under programmed decisions.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is a traditional technique for solving a programmed decision?
questions.explanation: The traditional techniques for handling a programmed (routine) decision include 'Habit', 'Clerical routine' (standard operating procedures), and 'Organization structure'. Judgment, intuition, and rules of thumb are traditional techniques for nonprogrammed decisions.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 183: Table 8.1 lists 'Habit' under Traditional Decision-Making Techniques for Programmed decisions.

questions.questionText: The person in the interpersonal communication situation who originates and encodes information to be shared with another person is called the ________
questions.explanation: In the interpersonal communication process, the source (or source/encoder) is the individual who originates and encodes the information that is to be shared with another person.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 348: "The source/encoder is the person in the interpersonal communication situation who originates and encodes information to be shared with others."

questions.questionText: Which of the following is a communication macrobarrier?
questions.explanation: A macrobarrier is a factor hindering communication in a general situation. The "need for increasingly complex information" is specifically listed as a communication macrobarrier. The other options are considered microbarriers, which hinder communication in specific situations.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 350-351: "The following are among the macrobarriers... 2. The need for increasingly complex information."

questions.questionText: ________ and ________ have been called the "Siamese twins of management."
questions.explanation: The textbook explicitly states that planning and control are virtually inseparable functions and refers to them as the Siamese twins of management.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 502: "As implied by this definition, planning and control are virtually inseparable functions. In fact, these two functions have been called the Siamese twins of management."

questions.questionText: The level of activity established to serve as a model for evaluating organizational performance is a(n) ________
questions.explanation: A standard acts as the yardstick that determines whether organizational performance is adequate or inadequate. The textbook defines it exactly as the level of activity established to serve as a model for evaluation.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 505: "A standard is the level of activity established to serve as a model for evaluating organizational performance."

questions.questionText: ____ is the management function through which managers compare present performance to preestablished performance standards.
questions.explanation: Controlling is the management function that utilizes a systematic effort to compare performance to predetermined standards to verify if performance is in line.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 502: "According to Roberto Mockler, controlling is a systematic effort by business management to compare performance to predetermined standards..."

questions.questionText: ____ includes determining tasks and groupings of work. It should not be rigid, but adaptable and flexible to meet challenges as circumstances change.
questions.explanation: Organizing inherently involves establishing tasks and forming departments (groupings of work). Henri Fayol advised that strict adherence to formal structures is not always advisable, highlighting that organizing should remain adaptable and flexible.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 261: "Fayol’s Advice on Using Formal Structure: Henri Fayol has indicated that strict adherence to a particular chain of command is not always advisable... organizations should be adaptable and flexible."

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is true for the management function?

options.content: It reaches goals by working with and through people.
It is a one-time function in an organization.
It is a series of continuing and unrelated activities.
It involves-and concentrates on reaching individual goals.
It does not consider the people aspect of organizations.

questions.explanation: The official definition of management states that it is the continuous process of reaching organizational goals by working with and through people and utilizing other organizational resources.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 6: "...management is the process of reaching organizational goals by working with and through people and other organizational resources."

questions.questionText: Name one of the basic concepts for smoothly running an organisation.

options.content: None of the options is correct

questions.explanation: Management is the overarching concept and process whose primary role is to smoothly guide the organization towards its goals by effectively utilizing its resources through the execution of its core functions.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 5: "Essentially, the role of managers is to guide organizations toward goal accomplishment... Management moves an organization toward its purposes or goals by assigning activities organization members perform."

questions.questionText: At what step of the planning can be processed with the topmost and applicable plan would be implemented?
questions.explanation: Despite the flawed wording in option 'c' ('Selecting up objective' instead of 'Selecting an alternative'), Step 4 of the planning process involves choosing the best alternative (the topmost/applicable plan) for implementation.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 164 (The Planning Process): "Step 4: Choose best alternative for reaching objectives."

questions.questionText: In which pace of the planning process, two sides of a coin of each alternative are examined.
questions.explanation: Evaluating alternatives involves analyzing the pros and cons (the two sides of the coin) of each listed option before making a final selection.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 164: Evaluating alternatives is the step where managers assess the benefits and drawbacks of each potential course of action.

questions.questionText: Thinking for the future output is known as

options.content: All of the options are correct

questions.explanation: Developing or setting planning premises involves making assumptions about the future conditions upon which alternative plans will be based. It is essentially thinking about future scenarios.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 164: Step 3 of the Planning Process involves "Develop premises upon which each alternative is based... a premise is an assumption upon which the alternative is based."

questions.questionText: Which of the following statements about groupthink is true?

options.content: Groupthink cannot take place in a newly formed group.
A group with high levels of mistrust and conflicts is likely to be most susceptible to groupthink.
A group with a high level of cohesiveness is likely to be susceptible to groupthink.
Managers must help committees to establish groupthink to generate alternative problem solutions.
A group which is highly competitive will most probably result in groupthink.

questions.explanation: Groupthink occurs when the "desire for agreement so dominates the group that it overrides the need to realistically appraise alternative problem solutions." High cohesiveness creates this strong desire for agreement and conformity, making the group highly susceptible to groupthink.
questions.reference: Chapter 18: Groups and Teams - Inferred from the definition and symptoms of Groupthink on Slide 16.

questions.questionText: At what stage of the planning becomes the most feasible plan that can be implemented.
questions.explanation: After evaluating options, the manager chooses the best (most feasible) alternative to reach the objectives, which is then developed into actionable plans.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 164: Step 4 of the Planning Process is "Choose best alternative for reaching objectives."

questions.questionText: Planning establishes a ground standard for
questions.explanation: Planning establishes the objectives and standards that the Controlling function then uses to measure actual performance against to determine if goals are being met.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 161 (and referenced in Control chapters): Planning is the basis for controlling, as controlling compares performance against the standards set during planning.

questions.questionText: _________ is the process of sharing information with other individuals.
questions.explanation: Communication is explicitly defined in the textbook as the process of sharing information with other individuals.
questions.reference: Textbook Page 348: "Communication is the process of sharing information with other individuals."

questions.questionText: A task group is a formal group outlined in the chain of command on an organization chart.
questions.explanation: A command group is defined as the formal group outlined in the chain of command on an organization chart. A task group is a formal group of organization members who interact with one another to accomplish most of the organization's nonroutine tasks.
questions.reference: Chapter 18: Groups and Teams - Found on Slide 9.
